county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records begin with county formation in 1897.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1897 from portions of York, Union, and Spartanburg counties. Pre-1897 records are split among these three parent counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cherokee is a young county (1897) so pre-formation research requires checking York, Union, and Spartanburg county records depending on the ancestor's location. The Cherokee County ROD (cherokeecountysc.gov/register-of-deeds/) offers remote access at cherokeencrod.org with indexes from January 1995 and scanned images from September 2002. The Cherokee Historical and Preservation Society (CHAPS, 301 College Drive, Gaffney, 864-489-3988, chaps@cherokeecountyhistory.org) is an active local resource. Marriage licenses on FamilySearch catalog #1056888 (1911-1950). The Pinckney District Chapter of SCGS (PO Box 5281, Spartanburg) covers Cherokee, Spartanburg, and Union counties and publishes the PDC Quarterly.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Indexes from January 1995; scanned images from September 2002. Remote access at cherokeencrod.org. Office: 110 Railroad Ave, Gaffney, SC 29340 (864-489-3988).
county_metadata.heritage_book: Cherokee County, South Carolina (Images of America series, Arcadia Publishing, 2003)

source_profiles.source_name: Cherokee County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indexes from January 1995; scanned images from September 2002. Remote access portal available. Pre-1897 deeds in York, Union, and Spartanburg counties.
FamilySearch catalog #1056888. Part of statewide SC marriage licensing from 1911.
Cherokee first appears in federal census in 1900 (formed 1897). Pre-1897 census records under York, Union, and Spartanburg counties.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Court records from 1785-1799 survive. Good survival rate for early period.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1785 from Camden District. Operated as a district until 1868.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chester has intact records and an active genealogical community. The Chester District Genealogical Society (PO Box 336, Richburg, SC 29729, 803-789-5664, Research@ChesterSCGenealogy.org) publishes a bulletin (FamilySearch catalog #386053). The Chester County Historical Society Museum (107 McAliley St., 803-385-2330, Wed 11am-3pm or by appointment) has extensive photo collections and genealogical materials. FamilySearch has county court minutes 1785-1799 (catalog #24622), marriage licenses 1911-1950 (catalog #982473), equity court records (catalog #253320), and cross index marriage register (catalog #989465). The Jean C. Agee Memorial Collection at Chester County Library has genealogical research materials. Heritage History of Chester County (2 vols., Tennessee Valley Publishers, 1982-1995) is a key reference.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Archived records available online through subscription service. Clerk of Court at 140 Main Street, Chester, SC 29706 (803-385-2605) maintains deeds, mortgages, plats, liens.
county_metadata.heritage_book: Heritage History of Chester County, South Carolina (2 vols., Tennessee Valley Publishers, 1982-1995)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #24622. Indexed early court minutes from county formation. Among the earliest surviving county court records in SC.
FamilySearch catalog #982473. Cross index marriage register also available (catalog #989465).
FamilySearch catalog #253320. Equity court records containing property disputes, family settlements, and estate matters.
All federal censuses from 1790 forward. Chester appears from the first US census. 1890 census mostly destroyed nationwide.

county_metadata.record_losses: Total loss of pre-1865 records. Courthouse records were evacuated to Columbia for safekeeping and destroyed in fires during Sherman's occupation February 17-18, 1865. One of six SC counties with TOTAL LOSS of all records prior to 1865. Deed books, court documents all destroyed. Estate papers and probate records from 1865 onward survive.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1785 from Cheraw District. Operated as a district until 1868.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chesterfield is one of SC's most severely burned counties — total pre-1865 record loss. Start with the State Land Grant Plats (1784-1868) at archivesindex.sc.gov, which specifically lists Chesterfield as a county that lost antebellum records. Estate records ca. 1865-1927 are on FamilySearch (catalog #956699). The Heritage of Chesterfield County (Tennessee Valley Publishers, c2004, 374 pages, FamilySearch catalog #1175778) contains family histories compiled by descendants. The Chesterfield Genealogical Society (PO Box 167, Chesterfield, SC 29709, chesterfield.scgen.org) meets 2nd Sunday monthly at the Old Chesterfield Courthouse and maintains a research library (est. Nov 2000) in the Old Courthouse with three rooms of materials including microfilm. Register of Deeds at chesterfieldcountysc.com/register-of-deeds/ (843-623-2172).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County portal with fee-based access for some records. Also available via sclandrecords.com. Office: Mill Street, Chesterfield, SC (843-623-2172).
county_metadata.heritage_book: Heritage of Chesterfield County, South Carolina (Tennessee Valley Publishers, c2004, 374 pages, FamilySearch catalog #1175778)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #956699. Estate records beginning after the 1865 destruction. The earliest surviving county-level records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: All pre-1865 records destroyed in Columbia burning. These post-war estate records are the foundation for Chesterfield research.

source_profiles.source_name: Plats for State Land Grants 1784-1868 (Chesterfield area)
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: State-level plats searchable at archivesindex.sc.gov. SCDAH specifically lists Chesterfield as a county that lost antebellum records, making these state plats a critical substitute for destroyed county deed records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-level records, unaffected by county record loss.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: All federal censuses from 1790 (Cheraw District) forward. 1890 mostly destroyed nationwide.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records, unaffected by county courthouse destruction.

county_metadata.record_losses: Records for Clarendon's predecessor jurisdictions (Claremont and Salem counties) were destroyed in the November 27, 1801 fire at the residence of Sumter District Clerk of Court John Horan in Stateburg. Probate records and deeds from the predecessor district period were consumed. Clarendon County itself (re-established 1855) has records from 1855 onward; probate records from 1865-1950 are microfilmed at SC Archives.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Complex history: original Clarendon County existed 1785-1800 (abolished), then re-established 1855 from Sumter District. The 1801 Sumter fire destroyed records for the original Clarendon, Claremont, and Salem counties. Current county seat is Manning.
county_metadata.research_tips: The 1801 Sumter District fire makes pre-1801 research very challenging for the Clarendon area. Post-1855 records at the county are generally intact. Probate records 1865-1950 are microfilmed at SC Archives; records 1951-2003 are not digitized; post-2004 records are scanned. The Clarendon County Historical Society and Museum (211 N Brooks Street, Manning, 803-433-0328) maintains local resources. Online deed search at acclaimprod.clarendoncountygov.org/acclaimweb (searchable by grantor/grantee, instrument number, document type, recording date, book/page). For pre-1855 research, check Sumter District records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Property records searchable by grantor/grantee name, instrument number, document type, recording date, book/page, case number, legal description. Official page: clarendoncountysc.gov/business-and-development/register-of-deeds/. Office: 411 Sunset Dr., Manning, SC 29102 (803-435-4444).

source_profiles.source_name: Clarendon County Register of Deeds Online
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Online deed search portal. Searchable by grantor/grantee name, recording date, book/page. Note: images not certified as true copies.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Post-1855 records intact. Pre-1801 records for predecessor jurisdictions destroyed in the 1801 Sumter District fire.

source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Microfilmed probate records at SC Archives. Post-1950 to 2003 not digitized. Post-2004 scanned in-house at county.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Pre-1801 probate records for predecessor jurisdictions (Claremont, Salem) destroyed in Sumter District fire.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Clarendon appears in federal census from 1860 (re-formed 1855). Pre-1855 census records under Sumter District/County.

county_metadata.record_losses: Virtually NO pre-1865 records survived. County records were evacuated to Columbia for safekeeping and destroyed in fires during Sherman's occupation February 17-18, 1865. Total loss of antebellum deeds, court records, and most civil documents. Post-1865 records survive: conveyance books (1866-1924), plat books, estate papers, will books, marriage books.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1785 from Colleton District. Parent county for Dorchester (1897, with Berkeley). Gullah/Geechee cultural heritage area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Colleton's near-total pre-1865 record loss makes it one of SC's most challenging counties. State Land Grant Plats (1784-1868) at archivesindex.sc.gov are specifically cited as critical for Colleton. The Colleton County deed search (search.colletondeeds.com) has deed books prior to August 1986 browsable free online, plus deed/mortgage indexes. The Old St. Bartholomew Chapter (~130 members) has recovered court dockets believed destroyed in the Civil War, plus a birth/death/marriage index from the Press and Standard newspaper. The Colleton Genealogy Society (colletongenealogysociety.org, chartered 2014) is active. The Colleton County Memorial Library South Carolina Room has extensive genealogical reference materials. Marriage licenses on FamilySearch catalog #1005261 (1911-1951, 15 microfilms). Church records: St. Bartholomew's Parish, St. John's Colleton Parish, St. Paul's Parish.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Deed books prior to August 1986 browsable free online. Deed index and mortgage index/books free. Post-1986 records in Colleton Real Estate Look-Up system. Office: 31 Klein Street, Harrelson Building, Room 115, Walterboro, SC 29488 (843-542-2745).
county_metadata.heritage_book: Colleton County, South Carolina - A History of the First 160 Years (1670-1830)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books prior to August 1986 browsable free. Deed/mortgage indexes free. Post-1986 in Colleton Real Estate Look-Up system (searchable by grantor/grantee, recording date, book, instrument number, type, map number).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Post-1865 deeds intact. All pre-1865 deeds destroyed in Columbia burning.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #1005261. 15 digitized microfilms.

source_profiles.source_name: Plats for State Land Grants 1784-1868 (Colleton area)
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: SCDAH specifically lists Colleton as a county that lost antebellum records. These state plats are a critical substitute for destroyed county deed records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: State-level records, unaffected by county record loss.

source_profiles.source_name: St. Bartholomew's, St. John's Colleton, and St. Paul's Parish Records
source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Colonial-era parish records from three parishes in the Colleton area. Particularly valuable because civil records pre-1865 were destroyed.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Church records survived the Civil War destruction of county records.

county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fire on March 19, 1806 destroyed most county records. Early probate files survived. Deed records do not exist prior to 1806; surviving deeds begin 1806. Deeds 1806-1869 and index to deeds 1806-1899 have been microfilmed.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1785 from Cheraw District. Operated as a district until 1868. Parent county for Dillon (1910) and contributed to Florence (1888).
county_metadata.research_tips: The 1806 fire means pre-1806 county records are almost entirely lost except for some early probate files. FamilySearch has Darlington County Records 1798-1928 as a digitized collection (collection #2144005). This includes coroner's records (1910-1986), Court of Common Pleas judgment rolls (1754-1977), Court of General Sessions records (1916-1986), probate records (1798-1923), Supreme Court records (1914-1960), and estates/wills/letters of administration (1791-1895). Note the 1754 start date for Common Pleas — some colonial-era court records survived. Deeds 1806-1869 are microfilmed with index through 1899. The 1800 census for Darlington District is on FamilySearch (catalog #733788). Online land records available through cotthosting.com/scdarlingtonexternal/.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Land records accessible through cotthosting.com/scdarlingtonexternal/ (county external portal). Deed records from 1806 forward (no pre-fire deeds). Index to deeds 1806-1899 microfilmed.

source_profiles.repository: FamilySearch / SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Microfilmed deeds beginning after the March 1806 courthouse fire. Index to deeds 1806-1899 also available. No deeds survive prior to 1806.
source_profiles.survival_notes: All pre-1806 deeds destroyed in courthouse fire.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch collection #2144005. Includes probate records (1798-1923), estates/wills/letters of administration (1791-1895), Court of Common Pleas judgment rolls (1754-1977), and coroner's records (1910-1986). The 1754 start date for Common Pleas suggests some colonial records survived.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Early probate files survived the 1806 fire. Some records predate the fire.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #733788. The 1800 census provides a pre-fire snapshot of Darlington District residents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal record, unaffected by county fire.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses. Records begin with county formation in 1910.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1910 from Marion County. Named for James W. Dillon (1826-1913), an Irish immigrant who brought the railroad to the area. Pre-1910 records in Marion County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dillon is a young county (1910) so all pre-formation research goes through Marion County. Probate Court has records from 1910; marriage records from 1911 (statewide mandate). Birth and death records from 1915 (statewide mandate) through Dillon County Health Department. For the Williamsburg District era, check SC Archives. The FamilySearch wiki page for Dillon County lists available collections. This area was part of the Pee Dee region with distinct Scots-Irish and English settlement patterns.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No free online deed portal found. Clerk of Court/Register of Deeds in Dillon, SC.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Probate records from county formation. Pre-1910 probate in Marion County.
Part of statewide SC marriage index.
Dillon first appears in federal census in 1920 (formed 1910). Pre-1910 census records under Marion County.

county_metadata.record_losses: No known major record losses for the county itself (formed 1897). However, parent county Colleton lost virtually all pre-1865 records in the Columbia burning, so pre-1897 records from the Colleton portion may have gaps.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created February 25, 1897 from Berkeley County and Colleton County. Named for the first settlement in the area, established by Congregationalists from Dorchester, Massachusetts in 1696. Pre-1897 records split between Berkeley and Colleton counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dorchester is young (1897) with pre-formation records split between Berkeley (intact) and Colleton (pre-1865 destroyed). Vital records: birth/death from 1915 (state mandate via Health Department), marriage from 1911 (Probate Court). For colonial-era research, the Old Dorchester State Historic Site preserves the ruins of the 1696 settlement. Check FamilySearch wiki for Dorchester County for specific collections. Church records from the Congregationalist settlement (1696+) may survive in Massachusetts archives as well as SC repositories.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No free online deed portal found. Register of Deeds at Dorchester County government offices, St. George.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Pre-1897 deeds split between Berkeley County (intact from 1882) and Colleton County (post-1865 only due to Civil War losses).
Part of statewide SC marriage index.
Dorchester first appears in federal census in 1900 (formed 1897). Pre-1897 census under Berkeley and Colleton counties.

county_metadata.record_losses: No major record losses. Edgefield has one of the OLDEST AND MOST COMPLETE collections of historical county records in South Carolina because the county NEVER suffered a courthouse fire AND General Sherman did not pass through the area during the Civil War. Records span from 1785 through modern times.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1785 from Ninety Six District. Major parent county: gave rise to Aiken (1871), Greenwood (1897), Saluda (1896), and McCormick (1916). Pre-1785 records in Ninety Six District at SC Archives.
county_metadata.research_tips: Edgefield is a genealogist's dream — complete records and no courthouse fires. The Edgefield County Archives (edgefieldcounty.sc.gov/archives/) maintains the county's remarkable historical collection spanning 1785 to late 20th century. Slave Records of Edgefield County by Gloria Ramsey Lucas (Edgefield County Historical Society, 2010, 432 pages, FamilySearch catalog #1911832) is a landmark publication with names arranged by owner. Ancestry.com has 'Edgefield, South Carolina, U.S., Slave Records, 1774-1866' (collection #60512). FamilySearch also has the Index to History of Edgefield County (catalog #354649) and Edgefield County cemeteries (catalog #687341). Because Edgefield is a parent of four counties, its records contain pre-formation data for large portions of western SC. The Old Edgefield District Genealogical Society (OEDGS, oedgs.org) is one of the most active societies in SC. The SCGS Archives are located at the Tompkins Memorial Library, 104 Courthouse Square, Edgefield, SC 29824 (803-637-4010).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Edgefield County Archives maintains records in-person. Clerk of Court at edgefieldclerkofcourt.org/archives has archival information. Contact for deed research.

source_profiles.source_name: Slave Records of Edgefield County, South Carolina
source_profiles.coverage_notes: FamilySearch catalog #1911832. Published by Gloria Ramsey Lucas (Edgefield County Historical Society, 2010, 432 pages). Names arranged by slaveholder/owner who sold the enslaved person. Landmark resource for African American genealogy in western SC. Also available on Ancestry.com (collection #60512).

source_profiles.repository: Edgefield County Archives / Clerk of Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete deed records from county formation in 1785. One of the most complete deed sequences in SC. Pre-1785 deeds in Ninety Six District records at SC Archives. Archives accessible at edgefieldcounty.sc.gov/archives/.

source_profiles.source_name: Edgefield County Will and Probate Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Complete probate records from county formation. Edgefield's unbroken probate record is particularly valuable because it covers the period before Aiken (1871), Greenwood (1897), Saluda (1896), and McCormick (1916) were carved out.
FamilySearch catalog #687341. Cemetery survey for Edgefield County.

county_metadata.record_losses: Fairfield County was in the path of Sherman's March (February 1865) and Winnsboro was occupied by federal troops. However, no documented courthouse fire has been confirmed for Fairfield through authoritative sources (FamilySearch wiki, LOC SC courthouse records guide, SCDAH county guides). Some Civil War-era damage and loss of individual records likely occurred, but the county is NOT listed among the officially recognized burned counties of SC. Researchers should be aware of possible gaps in the 1860s records.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1785 from Camden District. Operated as a district until 1868. Piedmont region with significant plantation history.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fairfield was in Sherman's path but the courthouse survived — it's not a confirmed burned county. However, expect some 1860s gaps. Check Camden District records for pre-1785 research at SC Archives. FamilySearch has county records collections. The county seat Winnsboro was a railroad junction and supply depot during the Civil War, making it strategically important. For enslaved persons research, plantation records from the Fairfield area may be at SC Archives or the South Caroliniana Library at USC. The FamilySearch wiki for Fairfield County lists available collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: No free online deed portal verified. Register of Deeds at Fairfield County government offices, Winnsboro.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from county formation. Not a confirmed burned county despite Sherman's passage through Winnsboro.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Generally intact. Some possible 1860s gaps but no confirmed total loss.

source_profiles.source_name: SC Archives Will Transcripts — Fairfield County
source_profiles.repository: SC Department of Archives and History
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Part of the SCDAH Will Transcripts 1782-1855 collection (21 counties). Searchable at archivesindex.sc.gov.
Part of statewide SC marriage index.
All federal censuses from 1790 forward. 1890 mostly destroyed nationwide.
